runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 9 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Pencavel (1986) - Labor supply elasticity estimates suggest modest substitution responses for prime-age males
Keane and Rogerson (2012) - Survey indicating substitution elasticities typically range 0.1-0.5 for male labor supply
MaCurdy (1981) - Intertemporal substitution elasticity framework foundational to wage-response modeling
runs.reasoningSummary: For primary earners in decile 9 (high earners), substitution elasticity is expected to be relatively modest. Higher earners typically face lower wage elasticity due to income effects and may have less flexibility in hours adjustment. The distribution reflects uncertainty around point estimates from the empirical labor supply literature, anchored by classical studies showing male labor supply substitution typically falls in the 0.1-0.5 range, with decile 9 likely on the lower end of this spectrum due to labor market constraints and income-dominated responses at higher earnings levels.

citations: Saez, Slemrod, & Giertz (2012) on elasticity of taxable income across income distribution
Chetty (2012) on behavioral responses to taxation and optimal income tax design
MaCurdy (1981) foundational work on intertemporal labor supply elasticities
runs.reasoningSummary: For primary earners in decile 9 (roughly 80th-90th percentile), substitution elasticity should be moderate but not trivial. Higher-income earners typically show elasticities in the 0.2–0.4 range based on classical labor supply literature. Decile 9 is below top earners (decile 10) who show larger responses, and above median earners. I center around 0.25 with asymmetric uncertainty reflecting that some micro-simulations report lower values (~0.1) while others find higher responses (~0.5+) depending on methodology and behavioral assumptions. The distribution is right-skewed to allow for heterogeneous responses and specification uncertainty.
